question: Why do you want to study physiotherapy?
lookingFor: A grounded, specific motivation backed by real exposure — not a generic desire to help people.

bullets: Identify the moment or experience that drew you to physiotherapy specifically
Show you understand the role involves physical rehabilitation, movement science, and patient relationships
Distinguish it from adjacent fields like medicine or nursing
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: My interest started when I supported my younger brother through his recovery from an ACL tear. I was struck by how the physiotherapist broke down complex movement problems into small, measurable goals — the science was precise but the relationship was everything. I started volunteering at a community rehab clinic to test that instinct, and watching patients regain function they'd been told they might not recover confirmed it. I want the combination of biomechanical problem-solving and long-term patient care that physiotherapy uniquely offers.

commonMistakes: 'I want to help people' with no substance behind it
Describing the job as doctors do — confusing the role with medicine
No real-world exposure to draw from
followUps: Was there a specific patient experience that stayed with you?

question: Why have you chosen our physiotherapy programme over others?
lookingFor: Specific knowledge of the course — clinical placement structure, specialist modules, or faculty research — not generic praise.

bullets: Name a concrete feature of this course: a placement partner, a specialism, a research strand
Connect it to a personal goal or gap you want to fill
Show you understand what makes this programme different
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I'm drawn to your early clinical placement model — most programmes wait until year two, but starting supervised contact with patients in year one matches the way I learn, which is by doing alongside theory. I also read that your department has a paediatric physiotherapy specialism, and after spending time volunteering at a children's disability service that's a route I want to keep open. I looked at several strong programmes but yours felt like the one built around the same philosophy I have about clinical learning.

commonMistakes: Naming only rankings or reputation with no specifics
Describing features that actually belong to a different university
Failing to connect the course features to personal goals
followUps: What would you contribute to our cohort?

question: Tell us about your relevant work or volunteering experience. What did you take from it?
lookingFor: Reflective insight drawn from real exposure — not a list of placements, but what you observed and what it changed.

bullets: Describe a specific setting and what you actually observed
Identify one thing that surprised or challenged your expectations
State what the experience confirmed or shifted about your motivation
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I spent three months observing in an NHS outpatient musculoskeletal clinic. The thing that surprised me most was how much of the physiotherapist's skill was communicative — getting a patient who'd been in chronic pain for years to trust that movement was safe again. I'd expected it to be more technique-heavy. It made me realise the psychological component of rehabilitation is inseparable from the physical, which I've since read into with Moseley's work on pain science. It deepened my interest in how beliefs about the body affect recovery.

commonMistakes: Listing placements without saying what you learned from any of them
Describing what physiotherapists do without reflecting on your own reaction
Choosing an experience too distant from physiotherapy to be relevant
followUps: Did anything you observed challenge your assumptions about the role?

question: How would you describe the role of a physiotherapist to someone who had no idea what it involved?
lookingFor: An accurate, jargon-free explanation that covers movement, rehabilitation, prevention, and patient autonomy — not just 'fixing injuries'.

bullets: Cover the breadth of the role: musculoskeletal, neurological, respiratory, paediatric settings
Emphasise restoration of function and independence, not just pain relief
Mention the educative and preventive dimensions
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: A physiotherapist helps people move better and recover function after injury, illness, or surgery — but the role is broader than most people realise. They work in hospitals with stroke patients relearning to walk, in sports clinics with athletes, in respiratory wards with post-surgical patients, and in community settings with children with developmental conditions. The goal isn't just reducing pain — it's building the patient's confidence and understanding of their own body so they can manage their health long-term. A lot of the work is educational and preventive, not just reactive.

commonMistakes: Describing only musculoskeletal or sports physiotherapy
Conflating physiotherapy with massage or chiropractic
Not mentioning patient education or self-management
followUps: Which of those settings interests you most, and why?

question: Why physiotherapy rather than medicine, nursing, or occupational therapy?
lookingFor: A clear, honest account of why this profession fits — showing understanding of all three alternatives without dismissing them.

bullets: Show genuine knowledge of what distinguishes physiotherapy from adjacent roles
Anchor the reason in a personal experience or value
Avoid implying the alternatives are inferior
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: All three are vocations I respect, and I thought about occupational therapy seriously because of the overlap in rehabilitation. What draws me to physiotherapy specifically is the depth of movement science — the anatomy, biomechanics, and neurophysiology sit at the centre of the discipline rather than at its edges. I'm also drawn to the hands-on continuity: physiotherapists often follow a patient across many sessions and build a treatment relationship over time. That combination of applied science and sustained therapeutic relationship isn't quite replicated anywhere else.

commonMistakes: Suggesting other caring professions are lesser
Giving a reason that applies equally to nursing or medicine
Showing no awareness of what occupational therapy or nursing actually involves
followUps: If you hadn't got into physiotherapy, what would you have done?

question: Can you walk us through what happens anatomically when someone sprains their ankle?
lookingFor: A confident lay-to-clinical explanation of ligament injury, grading, and healing — showing genuine pre-course reading.

bullets: Identify the most commonly affected ligaments (lateral complex, especially ATFL)
Describe grading of sprains: Grade I (stretch), II (partial tear), III (complete tear)
Briefly explain the inflammatory healing process and why early movement matters
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The most common ankle sprain is a lateral inversion injury — the foot rolls inward and the anterior talofibular ligament, and sometimes the calcaneofibular ligament, gets overstretched or torn. Sprains are graded from one to three: a Grade I is a stretch with no structural tear, Grade II involves a partial tear, and Grade III is a complete rupture. The immediate response is inflammation — increased blood flow, swelling, pain — which is the body starting repair. What I found interesting reading about this is that prolonged immobilisation actually slows healing because controlled early movement promotes collagen remodelling along the right lines of stress. That's part of why physiotherapy emphasises graded return to movement rather than complete rest.

commonMistakes: Describing only pain and swelling without the underlying anatomy
Confusing ligament and tendon injury
Not knowing the grading system or what it implies for treatment
followUps: Why do physiotherapists favour early movement rather than rest for most sprains?

question: What do you understand by the biopsychosocial model of pain, and why is it relevant to physiotherapy?
lookingFor: Understanding that pain is not purely structural — and that psychological and social factors shape the experience and recovery.

bullets: Define the three components: biological (tissue, nociception), psychological (beliefs, fear, mood), social (work, relationships, culture)
Explain why tissue damage and pain don't always correlate
Connect it to why physiotherapy addresses movement confidence and patient education alongside physical treatment
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The biopsychosocial model says pain is shaped by three interacting layers — the biological, which includes tissue damage and nerve sensitisation; the psychological, which covers fear of movement, catastrophising, and low mood; and the social, which includes work pressures, relationships, and cultural attitudes to pain. The reason this matters for physiotherapy is that two patients with identical MRI findings can have completely different experiences and recovery trajectories depending on those other factors. I came across Lorimer Moseley's work on pain neuroscience, and it shifted how I think about rehabilitation — helping someone understand that pain doesn't always mean damage, and that movement is safe, is sometimes the most important intervention a physiotherapist makes.

commonMistakes: Defining the model mechanically without connecting it to clinical practice
Treating pain as purely structural — ignoring psychological or social factors
Not knowing any key researchers or evidence in this area
followUps: How might you address a patient who is convinced that pain means further damage?

question: What role does physiotherapy play in the rehabilitation of stroke patients?
lookingFor: Awareness of neuroplasticity, task-specific training, and the multidisciplinary team — not just a general description of physiotherapy.

bullets: Explain that physiotherapy targets motor function, balance, and mobility after stroke
Mention neuroplasticity as the biological mechanism underpinning motor relearning
Note the role within the wider MDT alongside speech therapy, occupational therapy, and nursing
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: After a stroke, physiotherapy focuses on helping patients relearn movement — things like transferring from bed to chair, walking, and regaining arm function. The science behind this is neuroplasticity: the brain's ability to reorganise and form new pathways when given repeated, task-specific practice. So physiotherapists don't just exercise patients — they structure repetitive, meaningful movement tasks that drive cortical remapping. I find the early rehabilitation window particularly interesting because there's evidence that intensity in the first weeks matters enormously for long-term recovery. The physiotherapist works closely with speech therapists, occupational therapists, and nurses because stroke recovery touches every domain of function simultaneously.

commonMistakes: Describing stroke rehabilitation without mentioning neuroplasticity
Not acknowledging the multidisciplinary team context
Limiting physiotherapy to 'exercises' without explaining why structured movement matters
followUps: What do you understand by neuroplasticity, and how does it change what physiotherapists do?

question: Many applicants focus on musculoskeletal physiotherapy. What do you know about the respiratory side of the profession?
lookingFor: Awareness that physiotherapy extends well beyond sports and joints — and genuine curiosity about a less-obvious area.

bullets: Describe key respiratory physiotherapy contexts: post-surgical, COPD, cystic fibrosis, ICU
Mention techniques such as airway clearance, breathing re-education, and exercise rehabilitation
Show that this area interested you enough to explore beyond the obvious
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Respiratory physiotherapy is something I actively looked into because I didn't want to arrive thinking the job was mainly sports injuries. It includes helping patients clear secretions after surgery or with conditions like cystic fibrosis and bronchiectasis, re-educating breathing patterns, and running pulmonary rehabilitation programmes for patients with COPD. In ICU settings physiotherapists work on keeping ventilated patients mobile and reducing deconditioning. I was particularly struck by how critical the role becomes post-surgically — complications like pneumonia can be significantly reduced by early breathing exercises and mobilisation. It broadened my sense of how central movement science is even when the primary system is respiratory.

commonMistakes: Showing no awareness of physiotherapy beyond musculoskeletal and sports settings
Vague description without any specific conditions or techniques
Treating this as a lesser or unusual area rather than a core part of the profession
followUps: Which clinical area do you think you'd find most challenging, and why?

question: What does evidence-based practice mean to you, and why does it matter in physiotherapy?
lookingFor: Understanding that EBP integrates research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values — not just 'following the research'.

bullets: Define EBP as the integration of best available research, clinical expertise, and patient preferences
Give an example of where evidence has changed physiotherapy practice
Acknowledge that evidence has limits and clinical judgement still matters
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Evidence-based practice means combining the best available research with the clinician's expertise and the patient's own values and goals — it's all three, not just looking up what a study says. In physiotherapy this really matters because some traditional practices, like prolonged rest for low back pain, have been shown to worsen outcomes compared to graded activity. Reading about the shift away from passive treatments toward active rehabilitation over the last twenty years was what convinced me this is a discipline that takes its science seriously. At the same time I understand that evidence doesn't cover every patient scenario, and strong clinical reasoning still matters when you're in front of someone whose presentation doesn't fit the trial population.

commonMistakes: Defining EBP as simply 'following research guidelines'
Not mentioning patient values or clinical expertise
Unable to give any example from physiotherapy practice
followUps: Can you think of an area where the evidence in physiotherapy is still uncertain?

question: Physiotherapy requires patients to do uncomfortable work. How would you keep a patient motivated through a difficult rehabilitation?
lookingFor: Awareness of goal-setting, therapeutic alliance, and motivational strategies — not just cheerleading.

bullets: Mention shared goal-setting and making progress visible through measurable milestones
Describe the importance of understanding the patient's own values and what return to function means to them
Acknowledge that motivation fluctuates and a physiotherapist needs to adapt, not just push harder
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: From what I observed in clinic, the physiotherapists who got the best engagement were the ones who set goals in the patient's own language — not 'improve knee flexion by 20 degrees' but 'get back to playing football with your son.' Measuring and showing progress matters too: patients who can see they've done something they couldn't do two weeks ago are easier to keep on board. I also noticed that motivation isn't constant — there are hard weeks — and the best clinicians I watched adjusted their approach on those days rather than repeating the same script. Sometimes it's education, sometimes it's backing off slightly, sometimes it's just listening.

commonMistakes: Reducing patient motivation to encouragement and positivity
Not mentioning patient-centred goals or what function means to the individual
Suggesting you would simply increase exercise load when a patient is struggling
followUps: Have you ever had to motivate someone through a process they found difficult? What happened?

question: A patient tells you they've stopped doing their home exercises because they don't believe they're working. How do you respond?
lookingFor: Patient-centred reasoning that respects autonomy, explores the patient's perspective, and adapts rather than lectures.

bullets: Start by exploring why they don't believe the exercises are working — listen before advising
Check whether the programme needs adjusting based on what you hear
Respect their autonomy while sharing the evidence honestly
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: My first move would be to ask them what they've noticed — what made them feel the exercises weren't working? That might reveal something clinically important: either the progression has been too slow to produce feedback they can feel, or the exercises aren't right for their goals, or there's a psychological barrier I hadn't identified. I wouldn't lecture them about adherence. If after exploring it turned out the programme needed changing, I'd change it. If the evidence really does support the approach, I'd explain that evidence honestly — some gains take longer to feel than to measure. Ultimately, home exercise is their choice, and my job is to give them the best information, not to enforce compliance.

commonMistakes: Telling the patient they are wrong without exploring their perspective first
Ignoring the possibility that the programme itself needs adjustment
Treating adherence as the patient's obligation rather than a shared problem to solve
followUps: At what point, if any, would you escalate the situation or involve another professional?

question: What recent development or debate in physiotherapy has interested you, and what do you make of it?
lookingFor: Genuine engagement with the field beyond the textbook — a view, not just a summary.

bullets: Name a specific topic, paper, or development you have actually read about
Show you understand it well enough to have a view on it
Relate it to something in your own thinking or experience
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I've been reading about the growing evidence around telehealth physiotherapy — particularly for musculoskeletal conditions, where several RCTs now show outcomes comparable to in-person care for appropriate patients. What interests me is the equity argument: patients in rural areas, or those with caring responsibilities who struggle to attend clinic, now have a genuine option. The debate I find genuinely interesting is about the role of hands-on assessment — some experienced physios argue that remote consultation misses information you can only get by touching. I don't think I'm in a position to resolve that, but it seems like the profession is having to think carefully about which elements of treatment actually require physical presence and which don't.

commonMistakes: Naming a topic but being unable to say anything substantive about it
Summarising a single article with no personal reaction or view
Choosing something so general it suggests no real reading — 'technology in healthcare'
followUps: Where do you get most of your information about physiotherapy as a profession?

question: What attracts you to sports physiotherapy, if anything — and what would you say to someone who thinks physiotherapy is mainly about sport?
lookingFor: Honesty about personal interests plus a clear demonstration that the field is far broader than sport.

bullets: Acknowledge the appeal of sports physiotherapy if genuine — or honestly if it isn't your primary interest
Correct the misconception by naming other major clinical areas with confidence
Show breadth of awareness without dismissing sport as an area
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Sport is actually what first caught my attention — I played club rugby and saw physios in action at pitch-side. But when I started my volunteering I realised how narrow that picture was. The majority of the work I observed was NHS outpatient caseloads: chronic low back pain, post-operative hip replacements, elderly patients with balance problems. Sports physiotherapy is a specialism, not the centre of the profession. I'd say to someone with that assumption: the skills are the same — movement analysis, exercise prescription, patient education — but the populations are completely different, and most physiotherapists work in settings where the priority is independence and daily function, not athletic performance.

commonMistakes: Claiming interest in sports physio as the primary draw with no awareness of the broader profession
Being dismissive of sports physiotherapy when asked directly
Not being able to name two or three other major clinical areas
followUps: Which clinical population do you think you'd find most rewarding to work with long-term?

question: Can you explain the difference between a sprain and a strain, and give an example of each?
lookingFor: Precise, confident use of clinical terminology — ligaments vs muscles/tendons — with real examples.

bullets: Define sprain as injury to a ligament (bone to bone) and strain as injury to a muscle or tendon
Give a clinical example of each
Mention the shared grading system (I, II, III) if confident
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: A sprain is an injury to a ligament — the connective tissue that joins bone to bone. A classic example is a lateral ankle sprain, where the anterior talofibular ligament is overstretched or torn when the foot rolls inward. A strain is an injury to a muscle or its tendon — so a hamstring strain from sprinting is a muscle being loaded beyond its tensile capacity. Both are graded from one to three depending on severity: from a simple overstretching with intact structure, through partial tearing, to complete rupture. The distinction matters clinically because the healing timelines and management approaches differ — ligament injuries can affect joint stability in a way muscle injuries don't.

commonMistakes: Confusing the two — saying strains affect joints or sprains affect muscles
Not being able to give a clear example of each
Ignoring the clinical relevance of the distinction
followUps: Why might a complete Grade III tear sometimes be less painful immediately than a partial tear?

question: How would you handle a patient who becomes upset or tearful during a session?
lookingFor: Composure, empathy, and appropriate boundaries — the ability to hold space without overstepping clinical scope.

bullets: Pause the physical treatment and acknowledge what the patient is feeling
Create space without prying — ask if they want to talk or just take a moment
Know when to involve other professionals (psychology, social work) if needed
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I'd stop what we were doing. The exercise can wait. I'd acknowledge it directly — something like asking if they're alright or just sitting with them for a moment rather than jumping in with reassurance. People often become upset in physiotherapy for understandable reasons: they're in pain, they're scared they won't recover, or the process of rehabbing a serious injury is simply grinding. I'd let them lead on how much they wanted to share, and I'd listen without trying to fix it immediately. If it seemed like there was more going on — anxiety, depression — I'd raise it with my supervisor and we'd think about whether a referral to psychology or a social worker was appropriate. Physiotherapy is a team sport in that sense.

commonMistakes: Pushing through and continuing the session without addressing the emotion
Overstepping by trying to counsel the patient yourself
Immediately handing off without any attempt to acknowledge what the patient is feeling
followUps: Has a patient's emotional response ever surprised you during your experience or volunteering?

question: Why is multidisciplinary teamwork important in physiotherapy, and what does a physiotherapist contribute to the team?
lookingFor: Understanding of the MDT beyond a buzzword — specific roles, communication, and what physiotherapy distinctively brings.

bullets: Name key MDT members in a relevant context and describe their distinct roles
Explain what physiotherapy specifically contributes that other members don't
Describe the communication structures — ward rounds, case conferences — that make MDT work
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: In a stroke rehabilitation ward, for example, the physiotherapist sits alongside occupational therapists, speech and language therapists, nurses, neuropsychologists, and social workers — everyone is targeting a different domain of the patient's function. The physiotherapist's specific contribution is mobility, balance, and motor relearning. What makes MDT work effective is coordinated goal-setting so those efforts reinforce each other rather than pulling in different directions. From what I observed, the ward rounds and case conferences are where that coordination actually happens: you flag what you're seeing in your sessions, hear what others are noticing, and adjust the plan together. The physiotherapist has a distinctive voice in those conversations because they often have the most granular picture of how the patient moves.

commonMistakes: Using MDT as a buzzword without naming specific roles or functions
Not articulating what physiotherapy distinctively brings versus what any team member contributes
Describing MDT as simply being nice to other professionals
followUps: Can you give an example from your experience where two professionals' goals for a patient might conflict?

question: A patient has had persistent low back pain for two years. Their scans show no significant structural pathology. How do you make sense of that?
lookingFor: Confident engagement with pain neuroscience — understanding that persistent pain can exist in the absence of tissue damage and why.

bullets: Explain central sensitisation: the nervous system can amplify pain signals without ongoing tissue damage
Note that imaging findings often correlate poorly with pain experience
Describe what this means for physiotherapy management — graded exposure, pain education, addressing fear-avoidance
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: This is something I found genuinely fascinating to read about. In the early stages of injury, pain is usually a useful alarm signal tracking tissue damage. But when pain persists beyond healing time, the nervous system itself can become the problem — a process called central sensitisation, where pain pathways become hypersensitive. The scan showing no serious pathology is actually common in chronic low back pain; studies have found disc bulges and degeneration in large proportions of people with no pain at all. So the tissue isn't the driver anymore. For physiotherapy, this means the management approach is different: rather than targeting a structural problem that isn't there, the focus shifts to reducing the nervous system's sensitivity — through graded activity, explaining pain neuroscience, and addressing the fear-avoidance cycle where patients stop moving because movement feels dangerous.

commonMistakes: Suggesting the patient must be malingering or exaggerating if the scan is clear
Treating it as a purely psychological problem rather than a neurophysiological one
Having no awareness of central sensitisation or pain science
followUps: How would you explain central sensitisation to a patient in plain language?

question: Physiotherapy is a physically demanding profession. How do you look after your own physical health?
lookingFor: Self-awareness about the physical demands of the role and genuine habits — not a performance of wellness.

bullets: Describe a specific activity or routine that is genuine and sustainable
Show you have thought about the musculoskeletal risks of clinical physiotherapy (manual handling, posture)
Mention why self-care matters for sustaining a long career
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I've played team sport since secondary school and I've kept that up — at the moment it's weekly five-a-side and I try to run twice a week. I started thinking more deliberately about strength after reading about the prevalence of back problems in physiotherapists — the manual handling demands are significant, particularly in hospital settings. I've added some basic core and hip strengthening work because I don't want to arrive in clinic physically unprepared. I think this matters beyond just personal health — a physiotherapist who isn't managing their own body well is in a poor position to motivate patients to do the same.

commonMistakes: Vague answers about 'keeping active' without any specifics
No awareness that physiotherapy carries occupational injury risk for the clinician
Framing physical fitness as being for the patients' sake alone
followUps: What would you do if you developed a physical problem during your training?

question: A patient refuses a treatment you believe would significantly help them. What do you do?
lookingFor: Clear understanding of patient autonomy and informed consent — not paternalism, but also not passive acceptance without proper communication.

bullets: Establish that consent is always required and refusal must be respected in a competent adult
First explore the reason for refusal — it may reflect fear, misinformation, or a practical barrier
Offer alternatives, share information honestly, and document clearly
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: My starting point is that an informed adult has the right to refuse — that's not negotiable. But before accepting the refusal at face value I'd want to understand it: is it pain-related, does the patient not understand the rationale, have they had a bad experience with a similar treatment, or is there a practical barrier like the home exercise requiring equipment they don't have? Quite often the refusal carries useful information about what isn't working. If after an honest conversation they still decline, I respect that, document it clearly, and think about what we can offer instead. I'd raise it with a supervisor if I felt the situation was complex. Trying to pressure or persuade someone past their expressed wishes isn't physiotherapy — it's coercion.

commonMistakes: Suggesting you would simply override the refusal for the patient's own good
Accepting the refusal without exploring the reason behind it
Not mentioning documentation or the importance of a clear clinical record
followUps: Does your approach change if the patient lacks capacity to make the decision?

question: What particular challenges do you think working with children in physiotherapy presents compared to working with adults?
lookingFor: Thoughtful engagement with developmental variation, communication with parents, consent, and play-based approaches — not just 'they're smaller'.

bullets: Discuss consent and assent — parents consent, but children should be included in decision-making
Describe the challenge of assessment and treatment when a child can't describe symptoms reliably
Mention the role of play, the involvement of parents, and the importance of developmental norms
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The challenge that struck me most in my experience volunteering with a children's disability service was communication — a seven-year-old with cerebral palsy can't tell you in the same terms as an adult what hurts or what has changed. Assessment relies much more on observation, parental report, and standardised developmental tools. Consent is layered too: parents hold formal consent for young children, but good paediatric practice involves the child as much as possible in decisions — even at quite young ages you can build engagement through choice and play rather than instruction. Treatment approaches are also fundamentally different: exercises need to be embedded in games and meaningful activities, otherwise engagement collapses. And you're working constantly with parents who may have strong fears or hopes about their child's trajectory.

commonMistakes: Focusing only on the physical differences between children and adults
Not addressing the complexity of consent and the role of parents
Showing no awareness of developmental norms or how assessment differs
followUps: How would you handle a situation where a parent and a child had conflicting wishes about treatment?

question: How does physiotherapy contribute to healthy ageing, and why is this increasingly important?
lookingFor: Awareness of the ageing demographic, falls prevention, frailty, and the role of physiotherapy in maintaining independence.

bullets: Name specific physiotherapy contributions: falls prevention, strength and balance programmes, post-surgical recovery, managing chronic conditions
Connect to demographic trends — the ageing population and increasing multimorbidity
Describe the goal as maintaining independence and quality of life, not just treating acute episodes
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Physiotherapy has a critical role in helping older adults maintain independence — through falls prevention programmes, strength and balance training, pulmonary and cardiac rehabilitation, and managing the musculoskeletal sequelae of conditions like osteoporosis and arthritis. Falls are the single largest cause of injury-related death in people over 65, and physiotherapy-led balance programmes have strong evidence for reducing them. The reason this matters more every year is demographic: the UK population is ageing, multimorbidity is the norm in older patients, and the NHS doesn't have the capacity to manage frailty purely through acute medicine. Physiotherapy embedded in the community — helping people stay mobile, active, and at home longer — is one of the most cost-effective interventions there is. It's an area I find genuinely compelling because the outcomes are preventive, not just reactive.

commonMistakes: Describing physiotherapy for older adults only in terms of post-surgical or post-fall recovery
No awareness of the epidemiology of falls or frailty
Not connecting to the wider health system challenge of an ageing population
followUps: What do you think is the biggest barrier to older adults engaging with physiotherapy services?

subtopic: Scope of practice and professional limits
question: You are a student on placement. You notice a patient has a symptom that concerns you but your supervisor is busy. What do you do?
lookingFor: Clear understanding that student safety and patient safety take priority — the student never operates beyond their competence, and raises concerns appropriately.

bullets: Never proceed with treatment if there is a safety concern — pause and seek support
Interrupt the supervisor if necessary — a busy clinician is not a reason to ignore a red flag
Describe what you'd do if the supervisor was unavailable: escalate to another qualified clinician on site
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I'd stop. A student should never carry on with a treatment they have concerns about just because their supervisor is occupied. If the symptom suggested something potentially serious — a red flag like new neurological symptoms, chest pain, or sudden deterioration — I'd interrupt my supervisor. That's not an overreaction; it's exactly what patient safety requires. If my supervisor was genuinely unavailable and I thought the situation was urgent, I'd find another qualified clinician on site. If I was wrong, the worst outcome is a moment of interruption. If I was right and said nothing, the consequences could be severe. Placement is the time to err firmly on the side of caution and asking, not the time to demonstrate independence by staying silent.

commonMistakes: Continuing treatment and documenting the concern for later
Waiting until the supervisor became free before saying anything
Treating interrupting a busy supervisor as unacceptable or too presumptuous
followUps: What kind of symptoms would make you treat a situation as urgent enough to interrupt immediately?

question: What basic principles guide exercise prescription in physiotherapy, and why does progression matter?
lookingFor: Familiarity with overload, specificity, reversibility, and graded progression — not just 'do more exercises over time'.

bullets: Name core principles: overload (challenge beyond current capacity), specificity (match exercises to functional goals), reversibility (gains are lost without maintenance)
Explain why graded progression protects against re-injury while still stimulating adaptation
Connect to clinical application: starting where the patient is, not where the clinician thinks they should be
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The principles I've read about centre on overload — meaning the exercise needs to challenge the tissue or system beyond its current comfortable capacity to produce adaptation. Alongside that is specificity: strengthening a quad in isolation is not the same as training the movement pattern the patient actually needs to return to. And reversibility is the reason you can't just discharge and assume gains hold — the body deconditions if the stimulus is removed, which is why physiotherapy emphasises building habits that outlast the treatment episode. Progression matters because too much load too early re-injures tissue, and too little produces no adaptation at all. The skill is reading the patient — their pain response, their tissue stage, their confidence — and calibrating accordingly.

commonMistakes: Describing progression as simply 'adding more reps' without the underlying principles
Not mentioning specificity or reversibility
Treating exercise prescription as a generic process rather than individualised
followUps: How would you know when a patient is ready to progress to the next stage?

question: How do you learn from your mistakes, and how do you expect to use reflective practice as a physiotherapy student?
lookingFor: Genuine self-awareness and a practical approach to reflection — not just knowing what a reflective log is.

bullets: Describe a genuine example of learning from an error or a gap in your performance
Show understanding of what structured reflection adds — it forces you to examine why, not just what
Connect to the specific demands of clinical learning, where mistakes can affect patients
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I've found that I learn most from mistakes when I actually sit with the discomfort rather than moving on quickly. After a biology exam where I underperformed, I didn't just reread my notes — I went back through each question I'd got wrong and asked why I'd reasoned the way I had. That's where the actual gap was, not in the facts. In physiotherapy, where clinical errors carry real stakes, I think structured reflection becomes even more important: Gibbs or similar frameworks are useful not because they're a formula but because they force you to examine your reasoning and your emotional response, not just the outcome. I expect reflective logs on placement to feel uncomfortable sometimes, and I think that discomfort is probably the signal that the reflection is actually working.

commonMistakes: Describing reflective practice as a paperwork exercise rather than genuine learning
Not providing any personal example of learning from a mistake
Treating reflection as something that only happens after things go wrong
followUps: Can you give a specific example of something you've done differently after reflecting on an experience?
